{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question is whether progression of valvar aortic stenosis can be predicted accurately. From the retrieved documents, several key points support the ability to predict progression. Document [11] states that AVR is indicated in asymptomatic patients with severe aortic stenosis if there is evidence of rapid progression, defined as an annual progression of peak velocity greater than 0.3 m/s/year. This indicates that progression can be quantified and monitored. Document [21] notes that patients with stage C1 severe asymptomatic disease should have echocardiography every 6 to 12 months, implying regular monitoring to detect progression. Document [14] shows that the likelihood of requiring balloon valvuloplasty increases with higher gradients (e.g., 40% at 25–49 mm Hg, 70% above 50 mm Hg), indicating a clear correlation between gradient levels and progression risk. Document [23] states that surgical intervention is recommended when peak LVOT gradient exceeds 50 mm Hg, and decisions are based on symptoms and progression. These findings collectively demonstrate that progression can be predicted using measurable parameters such as pressure gradients, velocity, and annual progression rates. Therefore, the evidence supports that progression can be predicted accurately.", "answer_choice": "A"}
